clinicalStem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks' gestation is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting approximately 90 seconds. She has had no antenatal care. On arrival she is postictal but rousable. BP is 178/114 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, SpO2 94% on air. Urine dipstick shows 3+ proteinuria. Fetal CTG shows a fetal heart rate of 155 bpm with reduced variability. Which is the MOST IMMEDIATE next step in management?
options: Perform emergency caesarean section immediately
Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate loading dose
Start oral labetalol to lower blood pressure
Obtain urgent MRI brain to exclude intracranial haemorrhage
Arrange urgent ophthalmology review for fundoscopy
explanation: This is eclampsia — new-onset seizure in a woman with hypertension and proteinuria after 20 weeks' gestation. The immediate priority after airway/oxygen is IV magnesium sulfate (4 g loading dose over 5–10 min) to terminate ongoing seizure activity, prevent recurrence, and reduce the risk of maternal death. Delivery is indicated but only after maternal stabilisation; caesarean section as the very first action, before seizure control and stabilisation, risks catastrophic maternal deterioration.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Delivery is the definitive cure for eclampsia but must follow maternal stabilisation with magnesium sulfate and blood pressure control. Rushing to theatre before seizure prophylaxis and haemodynamic stabilisation is dangerous.

Correct. IV magnesium sulfate is the drug of choice for both treating an eclamptic seizure and preventing further seizures. This is the first pharmacological priority after establishing the airway and giving oxygen.

Antihypertensive therapy is important in eclampsia but oral labetalol is too slow for acute severe hypertension in an emergency. IV labetalol or hydralazine should be used. More critically, seizure control with magnesium takes priority.

MRI brain is not the immediate priority in a postictal eclamptic patient with a clear clinical diagnosis. Stabilisation and seizure prevention come first; imaging can be arranged if there is focal neurology or no response to magnesium.
Fundoscopy is part of a full clinical assessment in hypertensive disorders of pregnancy but is not an immediate emergency step. It will not alter the immediate management.
clinicalPearl: In eclampsia, give IV magnesium sulfate immediately after securing the airway — it reduces recurrent seizures by 50% and is superior to diazepam or phenytoin. Delivery comes after maternal stabilisation.
keyClue: Generalised seizure + BP 178/114 + 3+ proteinuria at 34 weeks = eclampsia requiring immediate magnesium sulfate
commonExamTrap: Choosing emergency caesarean section as the first step — delivery is the cure but maternal stabilisation with magnesium and antihypertensives must precede operative delivery.
missedConcept: Magnesium sulfate is the first-line anticonvulsant in eclampsia, not benzodiazepines; and stabilisation precedes delivery.
redFlagReason: Eclampsia is a life-threatening obstetric emergency with risk of maternal death from intracranial haemorrhage, aspiration, and cardiorespiratory arrest; immediate magnesium sulfate is critical.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman, G2P1, at 30 weeks' gestation attends her routine antenatal clinic. She is asymptomatic. Her booking BP at 10 weeks was 110/70 mmHg. Today her BP is 148/96 mmHg on two readings 4 hours apart. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein. She has mild pedal oedema. Platelet count is 165 × 10⁹/L, ALT 28 U/L, creatinine 68 µmol/L. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE diagnosis?
options: Gestational hypertension
Chronic hypertension
Pre-eclampsia
Physiological oedema of pregnancy
Superimposed pre-eclampsia on chronic hypertension
explanation: Pre-eclampsia is defined as new hypertension (BP ≥ 140/90 mmHg on two occasions) arising after 20 weeks' gestation in a previously normotensive woman, accompanied by proteinuria (≥300 mg/24 h or dipstick 2+ on a single sample in the appropriate context) or other end-organ involvement. This patient had a normal booking BP and now has BP 148/96 with 2+ proteinuria at 30 weeks — this fulfils the diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Gestational hypertension is new hypertension after 20 weeks WITHOUT significant proteinuria or end-organ involvement. The presence of 2+ proteinuria here excludes this diagnosis.
Chronic hypertension is hypertension present before 20 weeks or pre-pregnancy. Her booking BP was 110/70 mmHg at 10 weeks, so this is new-onset hypertension, not chronic.
Correct. New hypertension after 20 weeks in a previously normotensive woman, with significant proteinuria, meets the diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia.
Physiological oedema is common in pregnancy and does not include hypertension or proteinuria; oedema alone is not diagnostic of any hypertensive disorder.
Superimposed pre-eclampsia requires underlying chronic hypertension before 20 weeks, which is absent here — her booking BP was normal.
clinicalPearl: Pre-eclampsia = new hypertension (≥ 140/90 mmHg) after 20 weeks in a previously normotensive woman + significant proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction. Normal booking BP is essential context.
keyClue: Normal BP at booking (110/70) then BP 148/96 at 30 weeks with 2+ proteinuria
commonExamTrap: Labelling as gestational hypertension when proteinuria is present — proteinuria upgrades the diagnosis to pre-eclampsia.
missedConcept: Pre-eclampsia requires both new hypertension AND proteinuria (or end-organ feature); gestational hypertension lacks proteinuria.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman, G1P0, at 33 weeks' gestation is admitted with epigastric pain, nausea, and a frontal headache for 18 hours. She reports no visual disturbance. BP is 162/108 mmHg. Investigations show: Hb 9.8 g/dL (previously 11.2 g/dL), platelets 68 × 10⁹/L, ALT 245 U/L, AST 210 U/L, LDH 820 U/L, peripheral blood film shows schistocytes, and urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 450 mg/mmol. Which complication of pre-eclampsia has developed?
options: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy
HELLP syndrome
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Viral hepatitis
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
explanation: HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia. The diagnostic triad is microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (falling Hb, LDH >600, schistocytes on film), elevated liver enzymes (ALT/AST), and thrombocytopenia (platelets <100 × 10⁹/L). Epigastric pain reflects hepatic capsule distension. This patient fulfils all criteria in the context of pre-eclampsia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy also causes liver dysfunction in the third trimester but is associated with hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy, and hyperammonaemia; haemolysis with schistocytes is not a feature, and thrombocytopenia is usually milder.

Correct. The triad of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (falling Hb, LDH 820, schistocytes), severely elevated transaminases, and platelets 68 × 10⁹/L in the setting of pre-eclampsia is diagnostic of HELLP syndrome.
ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia without haemolysis or elevated liver enzymes, and is not specific to pregnancy-related hypertension.
Viral hepatitis causes elevated transaminases but not the microangiopathic haemolysis, schistocytes, or thrombocytopenia seen here, and is not associated with hypertension and proteinuria.

TTP presents with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic haemolysis but occurs outside pregnancy (or peripartum) and is associated with neurological features and severe renal failure; the clinical context here of pre-eclampsia makes HELLP more likely.
clinicalPearl: HELLP syndrome = Haemolysis (LDH >600, schistocytes) + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets (<100 × 10⁹/L) in the setting of pre-eclampsia; epigastric pain is the key symptom and carries risk of hepatic rupture.
keyClue: Falling Hb + schistocytes + elevated ALT/AST + platelets 68 in the context of hypertension and proteinuria
commonExamTrap: Missing HELLP because BP is not extremely high — HELLP can occur with relatively modest hypertension; look for the haematological and biochemical triad.
missedConcept: HELLP is a haematological and hepatic complication of pre-eclampsia; schistocytes on blood film confirm microangiopathic haemolysis.
redFlagReason: HELLP syndrome carries risk of hepatic rupture, DIC, placental abruption, and maternal death; urgent delivery is indicated after stabilisation.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding and dysmenorrhoea. She is parous (G3P3). Pelvic examination reveals a bulky, uniformly enlarged uterus approximately 12-week size with diffuse tenderness on palpation. The uterus is mobile. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a globular uterus measuring 10 × 8 × 7 cm with heterogeneous myometrial echotexture, asymmetric thickening of the posterior wall, and poorly defined echogenic islands within the myometrium. The endometrium is 6 mm and regular. Which is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Multiple uterine fibroids
Endometrial hyperplasia
Adenomyosis
Endometrial carcinoma
Pelvic congestion syndrome
explanation: Adenomyosis is the ectopic presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, causing a globular, uniformly enlarged uterus with diffuse tenderness. The classic transvaginal ultrasound features are heterogeneous myometrial echotexture, asymmetric myometrial thickening (typically posterior), poorly defined echogenic islands or cysts within the myometrium, and fan-shaped shadowing. The regular 6 mm endometrium argues against an endometrial pathology.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Uterine fibroids cause an irregular, lobulated uterine contour with discrete hypoechoic or heterogeneous nodules that are well-circumscribed and distort the uterine shape; the diffuse, asymmetric myometrial heterogeneity described here is more consistent with adenomyosis.

Endometrial hyperplasia presents as a thickened or irregular endometrium; it does not cause myometrial heterogeneity or a bulky uterus as the primary finding. The endometrium here is only 6 mm and regular.
Correct. Globular uterus + asymmetric posterior wall thickening + heterogeneous myometrial echotexture + echogenic islands on TVUS = adenomyosis. The classic TVUS criteria.

Endometrial carcinoma presents with endometrial thickening (>4 mm post-menopause or irregular endometrium), not primarily with myometrial heterogeneity. This patient's endometrium is 6 mm and regular.
Pelvic congestion syndrome causes chronic pelvic pain and dilated pelvic veins on imaging; it does not cause a bulky uterus with myometrial heterogeneity.
clinicalPearl: Adenomyosis on TVUS: globular uterus + asymmetric myometrial thickening + heterogeneous echotexture + echogenic myometrial islands. MRI is the gold standard if TVUS is inconclusive.
keyClue: Heterogeneous myometrial echotexture + asymmetric posterior wall thickening + poorly defined echogenic islands
commonExamTrap: Confusing adenomyosis with fibroids — fibroids produce discrete, well-circumscribed nodules; adenomyosis produces diffuse myometrial heterogeneity without discrete borders.
missedConcept: The TVUS hallmark of adenomyosis is diffuse myometrial heterogeneity with asymmetric thickening, not a focal mass.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a single episode of postmenopausal bleeding 3 weeks ago. Pelvic examination is unremarkable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 9 mm with a focal echogenic area measuring 12 mm within the endometrial cavity; the rest of the endometrium appears regular. Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle sampling) returns as insufficient tissue. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE next investigation to characterise the endometrial abnormality?
options: CT pelvis with contrast
Repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 6 weeks
Saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS)
Serum CA-125
MRI pelvis
explanation: Saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) — also called hydrosonography — instils saline into the endometrial cavity under ultrasound guidance, distending the cavity to outline focal intracavitary lesions such as polyps or submucosal fibroids. It is the ideal next step when TVUS identifies a focal endometrial lesion and Pipelle biopsy yields insufficient tissue, because it precisely characterises focal lesions and guides further management (e.g., hysteroscopic polypectomy).
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT pelvis does not have sufficient resolution to characterise focal intracavitary endometrial lesions and is not the investigation of choice for endometrial pathology.
Repeating TVUS in 6 weeks is inappropriate in a postmenopausal woman with bleeding and a focal endometrial lesion — this could delay diagnosis of pathology.

Correct. SIS provides excellent delineation of intracavitary focal lesions in a postmenopausal woman where Pipelle has failed. It distinguishes polyps from submucosal fibroids and guides hysteroscopic intervention.
Serum CA-125 is neither sensitive nor specific for endometrial polyps or early endometrial carcinoma; it has no role in characterising a focal endometrial lesion.

MRI pelvis is useful for staging confirmed endometrial cancer but is not the first-line investigation for characterising a focal endometrial lesion when SIS (a simpler, more accessible test) can achieve this.
clinicalPearl: Saline infusion sonohysterography is the best investigation for characterising focal intracavitary endometrial lesions (e.g., polyp vs submucosal fibroid) when TVUS identifies a focal abnormality.
keyClue: Focal echogenic intracavitary area on TVUS + insufficient Pipelle biopsy
commonExamTrap: Going straight to MRI when SIS would more directly and efficiently characterise an intracavitary focal lesion.
missedConcept: SIS distends the endometrial cavity with saline to outline focal lesions, making it superior to standard TVUS for intracavitary pathology characterisation.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman, G1P0, presents at 10 weeks by her LMP for a dating scan. She has irregular cycles ranging from 28 to 42 days. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy with a crown-rump length (CRL) of 48 mm, corresponding to a gestational age of 11 weeks and 3 days. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE action regarding gestational age dating?
options: Use the LMP-based gestational age as it is the gold standard in early pregnancy
Average the LMP date and the ultrasound date to estimate gestational age
Use the ultrasound CRL measurement to establish gestational age and revise the EDD
Request a serum beta-hCG to determine which dating method is more accurate
Repeat the ultrasound at 13 weeks to confirm dating before revising the EDD
explanation: First-trimester ultrasound CRL measurement (between 45–84 mm / 11+0 to 13+6 weeks) is the most accurate method for estimating gestational age and establishing the expected date of delivery (EDD), with a precision of ±5 days. LMP-based dating is unreliable when cycles are irregular, as in this case (28–42 day range). The CRL should be used to revise the EDD when there is a discrepancy, particularly when irregular cycles make LMP dating uncertain.
wrongAnswerExplanations: LMP-based dating assumes regular 28-day cycles. In women with irregular cycles (28–42 days as here), ovulation timing is unpredictable and LMP dating is unreliable — CRL is the preferred reference.
Averaging LMP and ultrasound dates has no clinical basis and introduces inaccuracy. The first-trimester CRL is the validated gold standard.
Correct. With irregular cycles making LMP dating unreliable, the ultrasound CRL at 11+3 weeks should be used to revise the EDD. CRL is accurate to ±5 days in the first trimester.
Serum beta-hCG does not establish gestational age precisely and plays no role in dating after a live intrauterine pregnancy has been confirmed on ultrasound.
There is no need to defer dating to 13 weeks — the current CRL of 48 mm at this visit is within the optimal dating window (CRL 45–84 mm) and provides reliable gestational age now.
clinicalPearl: First-trimester CRL (CRL 45–84 mm, 11+0 to 13+6 weeks) is the gold standard for gestational age dating, accurate to ±5 days — always use it over LMP in women with irregular cycles.
keyClue: Irregular cycles (28–42 days) + CRL giving a different gestational age than LMP
commonExamTrap: Defaulting to LMP dating despite documented irregular cycles — CRL supersedes LMP when cycles are irregular.
missedConcept: Irregular cycles invalidate LMP-based dating; first-trimester CRL is the accurate alternative.

clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman, G2P1, at 20 weeks' gestation undergoes her anomaly scan. The placenta is noted to be low-lying, with the lower placental edge 12 mm from the internal cervical os. She is asymptomatic. Her previous delivery was a normal vaginal birth. She is asking whether she will be able to have a vaginal birth this time. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE response and plan?
options: Advise caesarean section at 37 weeks as the placenta will not migrate
Explain that vaginal birth is contraindicated; plan elective caesarean at 34 weeks
Explain that most low-lying placentas at 20 weeks will migrate away from the os; arrange a repeat scan at 32 weeks
Discharge from follow-up as any placenta within 20 mm of the os is normal at 20 weeks
Refer for emergency cervical cerclage to prevent antepartum haemorrhage
explanation: At 20 weeks, up to 90% of placentas that appear low-lying will migrate away from the cervical os as the lower uterine segment develops between 20 and 36 weeks. A placenta 12 mm from the os at 20 weeks is unlikely to remain praevia at term. Current guidelines recommend a follow-up transvaginal ultrasound at 32 weeks (and again at 36 weeks if still low) to reassess placental position before advising on mode of delivery. Premature delivery decisions should not be made at 20 weeks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Advising caesarean section at 37 weeks at this stage is premature. The vast majority of low-lying placentas at 20 weeks resolve by 32–36 weeks as the lower uterine segment expands; a repeat scan is required first.
Planning elective caesarean at 34 weeks is inappropriate — this is pre-term and the placental position at 20 weeks is not a reliable predictor of placenta praevia at term. 90% will resolve.
Correct. Most low-lying placentas at the 20-week scan migrate upward by 32–36 weeks. The patient should be reassured and a follow-up scan arranged at 32 weeks to reassess.
Discharging from follow-up would be incorrect — a placenta 12 mm from the os at 20 weeks requires a repeat scan to confirm resolution, even though resolution is likely.
Cervical cerclage has no role in placenta praevia management and is not indicated here. It is used for cervical incompetence.
clinicalPearl: Low-lying placenta at 20 weeks resolves in ~90% of cases by 32–36 weeks due to lower uterine segment development. Arrange follow-up scan at 32 weeks before making delivery decisions.
keyClue: Placenta 12 mm from os at 20-week anomaly scan in an asymptomatic woman
commonExamTrap: Planning immediate caesarean delivery based on 20-week scan findings — placental migration means most low-lying placentas will resolve by 32 weeks.
missedConcept: Apparent placenta praevia at 20 weeks usually resolves by 32–36 weeks; follow-up scan, not immediate delivery planning, is the correct next step.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman, G1P0, at 41+3 weeks' gestation attends clinic for a post-dates review. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Ultrasound at 41 weeks shows adequate liquor volume (AFI 8 cm) and a cephalic presentation. CTG is reactive. Her Bishop score today is 4 (cervix unfavourable). She and her partner are keen to await spontaneous labour. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE advice and management plan?
options: Reassure and allow the pregnancy to continue until 43 weeks as the CTG is reactive
Offer immediate caesarean section as the Bishop score indicates an unfavourable cervix
Recommend induction of labour now, using cervical ripening with prostaglandins given the unfavourable cervix
Await spontaneous labour indefinitely; only intervene if CTG becomes non-reactive
Recommend amniotomy as the only method of induction at this gestational age
explanation: At 41+3 weeks, induction of labour is recommended by major guidelines (NICE, ACOG) because the risk of stillbirth increases significantly after 41 weeks — from approximately 1 in 3000 at 37 weeks to 1 in 500 at 42 weeks. With an unfavourable Bishop score (≤6), cervical ripening with prostaglandins (dinoprostone or misoprostol) is the appropriate method to ripen the cervix before or during induction. Awaiting spontaneous labour beyond 42 weeks exposes the fetus to increasing perinatal morbidity and mortality.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Allowing the pregnancy to continue to 43 weeks is not recommended by any major guideline; perinatal mortality risk rises substantially after 42 weeks and most guidelines recommend induction by 41–42 weeks.

An unfavourable Bishop score is an indication for cervical ripening before induction, not a reason to perform caesarean section, which carries significant maternal morbidity and is not indicated here.
Correct. At 41+3 weeks, induction is recommended; with an unfavourable cervix (Bishop score 4), prostaglandin-based cervical ripening is the appropriate method.
Awaiting spontaneous labour indefinitely beyond 41+3 weeks without a plan for induction is not safe practice — the risk of placental insufficiency and stillbirth increases significantly.

Amniotomy (artificial rupture of membranes) alone is ineffective with an unfavourable cervix; it requires a dilated, effaced cervix (typically Bishop score >8) and is not appropriate as the sole method here.
clinicalPearl: Post-dates pregnancy (>41 weeks): induction of labour is recommended; use cervical ripening with prostaglandins when Bishop score ≤6 (unfavourable cervix).
commonExamTrap: Choosing amniotomy for induction with an unfavourable cervix — ARoM requires a ripe cervix; prostaglandin ripening is needed first.
missedConcept: An unfavourable cervix (Bishop score ≤6) requires cervical ripening before or as part of induction; amniotomy alone is ineffective.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman, G2P1, at 40 weeks' gestation is being induced with IV oxytocin for prolonged rupture of membranes. She is on an oxytocin infusion at 8 mU/min. The CTG shows contractions occurring every 90 seconds, each lasting 75–90 seconds, with a fetal heart rate baseline of 140 bpm that drops to 80 bpm during each contraction with slow recovery. There have been 6 contractions in the last 10 minutes. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE immediate management?
options: Increase the oxytocin infusion to improve uterine efficiency and expedite delivery
Perform an immediate emergency caesarean section
Stop the oxytocin infusion, reposition the patient in left lateral position, and give facial oxygen
Administer IV morphine for uterine relaxation
Insert a fetal scalp electrode and continue current oxytocin rate
explanation: This is uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole) — more than 5 contractions per 10 minutes — complicated by prolonged late decelerations indicating fetal distress. The immediate management is to stop the oxytocin infusion (remove the cause), reposition the patient in the left lateral decubitus position (to relieve aortocaval compression and improve uteroplacental blood flow), and administer high-flow facial oxygen. If the fetal heart rate does not recover, tocolysis with terbutaline and preparation for delivery should follow.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing the oxytocin infusion would worsen hyperstimulation and deepen fetal hypoxia — this is dangerous and contraindicated.

Emergency caesarean section may ultimately be required if the CTG does not recover, but the immediate priority is to stop the oxytocin and attempt intrauterine resuscitation first. Rushing to theatre without conservative measures is not the first step.

Correct. The first immediate steps for oxytocin-induced hyperstimulation with fetal distress are: stop the oxytocin, left lateral positioning, and facial oxygen (intrauterine resuscitation). Terbutaline tocolysis can be used if the CTG does not recover.
IV morphine does not reliably cause uterine relaxation and is not indicated for managing hyperstimulation. Subcutaneous terbutaline is the tocolytic used in this context.

Inserting a fetal scalp electrode provides more accurate FHR monitoring but does not address the cause — continuing the current oxytocin rate while monitoring is dangerous when there is established hyperstimulation and fetal distress.
clinicalPearl: Oxytocin-induced uterine hyperstimulation with fetal distress: immediately stop the oxytocin, reposition left lateral, give oxygen — then consider terbutaline 0.25 mg SC if CTG does not recover.
keyClue: 6 contractions in 10 minutes + late decelerations + oxytocin infusion = hyperstimulation requiring immediate oxytocin cessation
commonExamTrap: Rushing to emergency caesarean before attempting intrauterine resuscitation — stopping the oxytocin and repositioning may resolve the fetal heart rate changes without operative delivery.
missedConcept: Uterine hyperstimulation is an iatrogenic emergency from oxytocin; the first intervention is to remove the cause (stop oxytocin) and perform intrauterine resuscitation.
redFlagReason: Uterine hyperstimulation causing fetal distress risks fetal hypoxia, acidosis, and stillbirth if not immediately managed; urgent cessation of oxytocin and intrauterine resuscitation are critical.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with 2 years of primary infertility. Her cycles are irregular, occurring every 35–60 days. She has a BMI of 26 kg/m². Investigations show: LH 12 IU/L, FSH 5 IU/L, testosterone 2.8 nmol/L (slightly elevated), prolactin normal, TSH normal. Transvaginal ultrasound shows bilateral ovaries with 15 antral follicles per ovary and ovarian volume 12 mL. Her husband's semen analysis and her tubal patency (hysterosalpingography) are normal. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE first-line pharmacological treatment for this patient?
options: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg on days 2–6 of the cycle
Letrozole 2.5 mg on days 2–6 of the cycle
Recombinant FSH injections starting at 75 IU daily
Metformin 500 mg twice daily as monotherapy
Combined oral contraceptive pill for cycle regulation followed by IVF
explanation: This patient has polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) — irregular cycles, elevated LH:FSH ratio, mild hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound — with anovulatory infertility. Letrozole (an aromatase inhibitor) is now recommended as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS-related anovulatory infertility by NICE (2023) and ASRM/ESHRE (2023) because it achieves higher live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy rates compared to clomiphene citrate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Clomiphene citrate was previously first-line for PCOS anovulation but is now superseded by letrozole based on RCT evidence (Legro et al. NEJM 2014) showing higher live birth rates with letrozole. Clomiphene is a reasonable second-line alternative if letrozole is unavailable.

Correct. Letrozole is now the recommended first-line ovulation induction agent for PCOS-related anovulatory infertility based on current guidelines, with superior live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy risk versus clomiphene.

Gonadotropin injections (FSH) are second-line therapy for anovulatory infertility in PCOS when oral agents fail; they require intensive ultrasound monitoring and carry significant risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) and multiple pregnancy.

Metformin monotherapy is not recommended as a first-line ovulation induction agent; it can improve cycle regularity but is less effective than letrozole for inducing ovulation and achieving live birth.
The oral contraceptive pill regulates cycles but does not induce ovulation for conception; proceeding directly to IVF without attempting ovulation induction is inappropriate at this stage.
clinicalPearl: Letrozole is the first-line ovulation induction agent in PCOS-related anovulatory infertility — it outperforms clomiphene in live birth rates and carries lower multiple pregnancy risk.
keyClue: Irregular cycles + polycystic ovarian morphology + LH:FSH ratio >2 + mild hyperandrogenism = PCOS anovulation; letrozole first-line
commonExamTrap: Choosing clomiphene citrate as first-line — current guidelines (NICE 2023, ASRM/ESHRE 2023) now favour letrozole over clomiphene for PCOS.
missedConcept: Letrozole has replaced clomiphene as first-line pharmacological ovulation induction for PCOS based on superior live birth rate data.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man attends a fertility clinic with his 32-year-old partner after 18 months of unprotected sexual intercourse without conception. His partner's investigations are all normal, including regular ovulatory cycles, normal tubal patency, and normal hormonal profile. His semen analysis results are: volume 2.5 mL, sperm concentration 12 × 10⁶/mL (reference >16 × 10⁶/mL), total motility 45% (reference >42%), progressive motility 32% (reference >30%), normal morphology 2% (reference >4%). Which is the MOST ACCURATE characterisation of this semen analysis?
options: Normal semen analysis — no male factor identified
Oligozoospermia only
Oligoteratozoospermia
Azoospermia
Asthenoteratozoospermia
explanation: WHO 2021 reference values define: oligozoospermia (concentration <16 × 10⁶/mL), teratozoospermia (normal morphology <4%), and asthenozoospermia (total motility <42% or progressive motility <30%). This patient has a low concentration (12 × 10⁶/mL) and abnormal morphology (2%), with motility within normal limits. The correct term is oligoteratozoospermia — a combined defect in count and morphology. The 'OAT syndrome' refers to combined oligo-astheno-teratozoospermia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The semen analysis is not normal. Concentration is below the WHO 2021 reference (12 vs. 16 × 10⁶/mL) and morphology is subnormal (2% vs. 4%). At least two parameters are abnormal.
Oligozoospermia alone would mean only the sperm count is below reference values. Here, morphology is also abnormal (2% normal forms), so the correct combined term is oligoteratozoospermia.

Correct. Oligozoospermia (concentration 12 × 10⁶/mL, below WHO 2021 threshold of 16) combined with teratozoospermia (2% normal morphology, below threshold of 4%) = oligoteratozoospermia. Motility is within normal limits.
Azoospermia means the complete absence of sperm in the ejaculate. This patient has 12 × 10⁶/mL sperm — oligozoospermia, not azoospermia.

Asthenoteratozoospermia refers to combined abnormal motility and morphology. This patient's motility parameters are within normal limits (total motility 45%, progressive 32%), so astheno- prefix does not apply.
clinicalPearl: WHO 2021 lower reference limits: concentration >16 × 10⁶/mL, total motility >42%, normal morphology >4%. Oligoteratozoospermia = low count + abnormal morphology with normal motility.
commonExamTrap: Calling it OAT (oligo-astheno-teratozoospermia) when motility is actually normal — only label astheno- if motility is genuinely below the reference.
missedConcept: WHO 2021 updated the lower reference limit for concentration to 16 × 10⁶/mL (previously 15); morphology threshold remains 4% normal forms.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman, nulliparous, presents to the fertility clinic after 3 years of primary infertility. Her partner's semen analysis is normal. Hysterosalpingography shows a left-sided hydrosalpinx with spillage on the right side. Transvaginal ultrasound confirms a 35 mm anechoic left adnexal structure consistent with hydrosalpinx. Her ovarian reserve is normal (AMH 18 pmol/L). She has had two cycles of intrauterine insemination (IUI) without success. The team is planning IVF. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE advice regarding the hydrosalpinx prior to IVF?
options: Proceed directly to IVF — a unilateral hydrosalpinx does not affect implantation rates
Advise laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube prior to IVF to improve implantation rates
Drain the hydrosalpinx transvaginally under ultrasound guidance just before embryo transfer
Prescribe a course of antibiotics to treat the hydrosalpinx before IVF
Perform hysteroscopic tubal occlusion of the left tube as the preferred surgical option before IVF
explanation: Hydrosalpinx significantly reduces IVF implantation and live birth rates by approximately 50%, due to retrograde leakage of toxic hydrosalpinx fluid into the endometrial cavity impairing embryo implantation. Laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube prior to IVF is the recommended management (NICE, ESHRE guidelines) as it removes the toxic fluid reservoir and substantially improves IVF outcomes. Salpingectomy does not compromise ovarian blood supply when performed carefully.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A hydrosalpinx substantially reduces IVF implantation rates by ~50% through toxic fluid reflux. Proceeding without treatment would significantly compromise the chance of IVF success.

Correct. Laparoscopic salpingectomy prior to IVF is the recommended treatment for hydrosalpinx to improve implantation rates. It removes the source of embryotoxic fluid and is supported by randomised trial evidence.
Transvaginal aspiration of hydrosalpinx fluid is not recommended — the fluid rapidly re-accumulates before embryo transfer, so this approach does not provide sustained benefit.
Antibiotics do not treat a structural hydrosalpinx; the issue is mechanical obstruction and toxic fluid accumulation, not active infection. Antibiotics have no role.

Hysteroscopic tubal occlusion is an alternative when laparoscopy is contraindicated or technically impossible; however, salpingectomy is preferred as it completely removes the tube and its contents and is the first-choice approach.
clinicalPearl: Hydrosalpinx reduces IVF live birth rates by ~50%; laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube before IVF is recommended to restore implantation rates.
keyClue: Confirmed hydrosalpinx on ultrasound + planning IVF = salpingectomy recommended before embryo transfer
commonExamTrap: Choosing transvaginal drainage — the fluid re-accumulates rapidly and this approach has not been shown to consistently improve IVF outcomes.
missedConcept: Hydrosalpinx fluid is embryotoxic and impairs endometrial receptivity; salpingectomy removes the source and is the evidence-based intervention before IVF.
